skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation for AI agents via inference.sh. Navigate web pages, interact with elements using @e refs, take screenshots, record video. Capabilities: web scraping, form filling, clicking, typing, drag-drop, file upload, JavaScript execution. Use for: web automation, data extraction, testing, agent browsing, research. Triggers: browser, web automation, scrape, navigate, click, fill form, screenshot, browse web, playwright, headless browser, web agent, surf internet, record video
skill.summary: Drive a remote Chromium browser from Claude or Codex through the inference.sh belt CLI: open pages, click and fill forms by @e refs, run JavaScript, take screenshots, and record video.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 607 patterns, but manual review confirms the skill is a documentation wrapper for the legitimate inference.sh belt CLI. The 502 'shell backtick' hits are bash variable-interpolation examples (the '"$VAR"' idiom for embedding shell variables inside single-quoted JSON), 62 'hardcoded URL' hits are documentation placeholders like https://example.com plus the inference.sh service the skill is built on, and 18 'path traversal' hits are markdown cross-references such as [session-management.md](session-management.md) and ../SKILL.md. The CRITICAL 'browser credential files' and 'dangerous combination' flags are heuristic false positives: the session-management.md text only describes that cookies and LocalStorage persist within a session, it does not access them; and any browser automation tool necessarily combines network, filesystem, and command execution. The HIGH 'weak crypto' hits are oathtool --totp invocations (RFC 6238, the standard 2FA mechanism). The skill wraps a third-party service, executes arbitrary JavaScript on user-specified URLs, and can upload local files, so it warrants a medium risk rating but is not malicious.

high_findings.title: Skill routes browser traffic through a third-party service
high_findings.description: The skill does not run a local browser. Every page load, JavaScript execution result, screenshot, and uploaded file is sent to the inference.sh remote Chromium service via the belt CLI. Users with sensitive data, proprietary code, or confidential credentials should treat inference.sh as a trusted third party before invoking the skill on internal sites. The documentation does not disclose the data-handling policy of inference.sh.

medium_findings.title: Skill executes arbitrary JavaScript on user-specified URLs
medium_findings.description: The execute function (documented in references/commands.md) runs JavaScript provided in the input JSON against the loaded page. While the page is sandboxed by the browser, the JavaScript string itself is interpreted by inference.sh, and any data it can read on the page (cookies visible to JS, DOM, network responses) is returned to the caller. This is a documented feature, not malicious, but it expands the attack surface beyond simple click/fill automation and means the skill can exfiltrate page content from any site the user authorizes.

medium_findings.title: Skill can upload local files to arbitrary web forms
medium_findings.description: The upload action (documented in references/commands.md) takes local file_paths and submits them to file inputs on the loaded page. Combined with a JavaScript execute call, the user can chain a file read with a network upload. The default trust boundary is whatever the user sets, but the skill provides the mechanism.

medium_findings.title: SOCKS5 and authenticated proxy support enable geo-spoofing and traffic redirection
medium_findings.description: The proxy support allows HTTP, HTTPS, and SOCKS5 proxies with username/password authentication. While legitimate for geo-testing and corporate access, it also means the skill can be used to route all session traffic through arbitrary user-chosen endpoints, including exit nodes the user does not control.

low_findings.title: Video recording may capture sensitive on-screen content
low_findings.description: The record_video option (documented in references/video-recording.md) captures everything visible in the browser, including typed passwords and form data. The skill itself recommends disabling recording for sensitive sessions, but users who enable it for debugging will incidentally record credentials.

low_findings.title: Skill includes a TOTP generation workflow that uses oathtool
low_findings.description: authentication.md shows how to fill a 2FA code generated by oathtool --totp -b. The TOTP secret is read from $TOTP_SECRET in the user environment. TOTP (RFC 6238) is a standard, trusted 2FA mechanism; this finding is informational only and not a security weakness.

dangerous_patterns.title: Heuristic: code execution + network + credential context
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged the combination of shell command execution, network access, and credential-handling prose as a dangerous pattern. Manual review confirms the combination is intrinsic to a browser automation skill: the belt CLI must be invoked (command), must contact inference.sh (network), and references session cookies/credentials because that is what the browser stores. There is no obfuscation, no covert exfiltration, and no unauthorized behavior. The finding is a heuristic false positive.

content.value_statement: Browser automation lets AI agents navigate the web without writing Playwright code. The agent-browser skill teaches Claude and Codex to drive a remote Chromium session through the inference.sh belt CLI, capturing screenshots, video, and structured page state.
actual_capabilities: Open any URL and receive a compact snapshot of interactive elements with @e references
Click, type, fill, drag, drop, hover, select, check, scroll, and press keys against element refs
Take viewport or full-page screenshots, run arbitrary JavaScript on the loaded page, and read the result
Record the entire session as a WebM video that is returned on close
Persist cookies, LocalStorage, SessionStorage, and IndexedDB across multiple belt calls and reuse a session id
limitations: Requires the inference.sh belt or infsh CLI installed and authenticated on the host
Sessions do not survive inference.sh server restarts and there is no automatic session recovery
Snapshots are limited to 50 interactive elements per page, so dense pages require scrolling or custom JavaScript
All browser traffic, page content, and uploaded files pass through the inference.sh remote service

use_cases.title: Web research and data extraction
use_cases.description: Open a target site, take a snapshot to find the right elements, then use the execute function or interact to pull structured text, links, and tables. Save the output to a file with jq or to a screenshot for citations.

use_cases.title: End-to-end browser testing in CI
use_cases.description: Run logins, form submissions, and UI assertions through Claude or Codex, record video as CI evidence, and run multiple sessions in parallel from different proxies for cross-region coverage.

use_cases.title: Internal portal automation and CRM updates
use_cases.description: Log in to a web app with the authenticated-session template, navigate to a form, fill it with data from another tool, and submit. Useful for back-office workflows that lack an API.

prompt_templates.title: Open a page and read its elements
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com in a new browser session and tell me the text of the first 5 interactive elements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: first contact with the skill, no login required

prompt_templates.title: Log in and capture an authenticated page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://app.example.com/login, fill the email field with $APP_USERNAME and the password field with $APP_PASSWORD, click Sign In, wait 3 seconds, and take a full-page screenshot. Use the env vars, do not hardcode credentials.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: authenticated browsing with environment variables

prompt_templates.title: Scrape a paginated catalog to CSV
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://shop.example.com/catalog, scroll to the bottom three times to load more results, then run JavaScript to extract the product name and price of every visible item. Save the result as products.csv in the current directory.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: data extraction with custom JavaScript and file output

prompt_templates.title: Geo-test a site from three regions in parallel
prompt_templates.prompt: Start three parallel browser sessions through US, EU, and Asia SOCKS5 proxies, open https://mysite.com in each, take a full-page screenshot, and report any differences in the rendered hero text.
prompt_templates.scenario: Power user: parallel sessions and proxy rotation

output_examples.input: Open https://example.com and list the interactive elements I can act on.
output_examples.output: The session opens the page and returns a snapshot. You can act on: Home link, More information link, and any form fields on the page. A short elements_text is shown: @e1 [a] "Home" href="/" — @e2 [a] "More information..." href="https://www.iana.org/domains/example" — and the skill returns a screenshot file path.

output_examples.input: Log in to my dashboard and take a full-page screenshot.
output_examples.output: The skill starts a new session, fills the email field with $APP_USERNAME, fills the password field with $APP_PASSWORD, clicks Sign In, waits for the redirect, then captures a full-page screenshot. The close response includes the final URL and the PNG file path.

output_examples.input: Record a video of me using the site so I can share it with my team.
output_examples.output: The skill opens the session with record_video enabled, performs the requested actions, and on close returns a WebM file path. The recording captures the entire session including cursor movements and click feedback.

best_practices: Store the session id from the first call and pass it to every subsequent call instead of opening a new session per action, to keep cookies and storage consistent
Re-snapshot after every navigation or DOM change because @e references are invalidated when the page transitions, and the interact response already includes a fresh snapshot
Keep credentials and proxy authentication in environment variables, read them with ${VAR}, and never echo them in plain text or include them in recorded video sessions
anti_patterns: Hardcoding passwords, proxy usernames, or proxy passwords in shell scripts instead of reading them from env vars or a secrets manager
Enabling record_video on sessions that handle real credentials, since the recording will capture typed passwords and on-screen tokens
Calling snapshot, click, snapshot in a tight loop when the interact response already returns a fresh snapshot, which doubles the round-trip cost without benefit

faq.question: Does the agent-browser skill run a browser on my machine?
faq.answer: No. The skill drives a remote Chromium instance provided by inference.sh through the belt or infsh CLI. Your local machine only sends commands and receives screenshots, text, and video.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything besides this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. You need the inference.sh belt (or infsh) CLI installed and authenticated with your account. The skill is documentation and reference material, not a self-contained browser.

faq.question: Can I point the skill at my own Playwright server?
faq.answer: No. The skill is built around the inference.sh agent-browser app and its belt CLI interface. Customizing it for another backend would require rewriting the reference material.

faq.question: What happens if my session times out?
faq.answer: Subsequent calls return a 'session not found' error. You must open a new session and re-authenticate, because sessions do not persist across inference.sh server restarts and there is no automatic recovery.

faq.question: Is any of my data sent to inference.sh?
faq.answer: Yes. Every URL you open, every JavaScript string you ask the agent to execute, every form value you fill, and every local file you upload is sent to the inference.sh service that runs the browser. Treat inference.sh as a trusted third party before using the skill on internal or sensitive sites.

faq.question: How does the skill handle two-factor authentication?
faq.answer: It can fill a TOTP code generated by oathtool --totp, or pause for manual entry of SMS or hardware-token codes. The manual-entry path uses record_video so a human can see the prompt and type the code.
